## Logistic regression is a classifier, not a ranking trick

Despite the name, logistic regression is a classification model. It starts with the
same linear predictor idea as ordinary linear regression, but maps that score into a
probability between `0` and `1`.

That makes it a strong baseline for binary outcomes in `TEM 0052`: transparent,
reasonably interpretable, and easy to compare against more flexible classifiers.

## From linear score to probability

Logistic regression uses a linear score

$$
z_i = \beta_0 + x_i^\top\beta
$$

and transforms it with the logistic function

$$
\Pr(y_i = 1 \mid x_i) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z_i}}.
$$

This matters because predictions now have a probabilistic interpretation. We are not
just assigning class labels. We are estimating the chance of the positive class under
the model.

## What the coefficients mean

The coefficients still describe a linear relationship, but now on the log-odds scale:

$$
\log\left(\frac{p_i}{1 - p_i}\right) = \beta_0 + x_i^\top\beta.
$$

In practice, students do not need to become log-odds purists. They do need to know
three things:

- coefficient signs still matter,
- feature scaling can strongly affect optimization and coefficient comparison,
- and the same coefficient can look reasonable while the classification threshold is still wrong for the task.

## Thresholds and metrics are part of the model choice

The default `0.5` threshold is only a starting point. The right threshold depends on
the error trade-off in the application.

That is why evaluation should not stop at overall accuracy. In this course, a
logistic workflow should usually check:

- confusion matrix structure,
- precision and recall where class balance matters,
- and whether predicted probabilities appear well calibrated enough to trust.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students whether a model with slightly lower accuracy but much better recall for
the positive class might be the better choice in a business or policy setting.
:::

## Where logistic regression fits in `TEM 0052`

Logistic regression belongs early in the course because it connects several core
ideas:

- the linear-model logic from ordinary regression,
- the model-selection discipline from cross-validation,
- and the classification workflow used later for trees, ensembles, and support vector machines.

It is also one of the baseline models in the model-assessment lab, where students use
the same resampling rule to compare logistic regression with alternatives.

## What to report

When students present a logistic-regression workflow in `TEM 0052`, they should
usually report:

- the target definition and class balance,
- the preprocessing and feature-coding rule,
- the train/test or cross-validation protocol,
- at least one threshold-free or probability-aware evaluation point together with class predictions,
- and one short explanation of whether the model is useful because it is accurate,
  interpretable, well-calibrated, or simply a strong baseline.
